perturbation.original_text: Supplier shall provide the Maintenance Services described in this for Software, Updates and Upgrades provided to Bank of America pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Supplier shall endeavor to provide the Maintenance Services described in this for Software, Updates and Upgrades provided to Bank of America pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall provide' to 'shall endeavor to provide' weakens the obligation, making the provision of maintenance services less mandatory and more subject to Supplier's discretion. The term 'endeavor' is ambiguous and does not define the level of effort required.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Failure to Provide Essential Maintenance Services
perturbation.law_explanation: 12 CFR Part 30 Appendix B requires banking institutions to have a process to ensure third-party service providers deliver adequate services. By weakening the language around providing maintenance services, it becomes ambiguous whether the service provider is truly obligated to meet those service levels, potentially resulting in a failure to provide necessary maintenance, which can affect a bank's operations and compliance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall provide' to 'shall endeavor to provide' introduces an ambiguity that weakens the supplier's obligation to provide maintenance services. According to 12 CFR Part 30 Appendix B, banking institutions must ensure third-party service providers deliver adequate services. The modified text makes it unclear if the supplier is truly obligated to meet the required service levels, potentially leading to inadequate maintenance. The term 'endeavor' doesn't define a specific level of effort, making it difficult to enforce the original intent of providing necessary maintenance, affecting the bank's operations and compliance, creating an Ambiguities Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Supplier shall make reasonable efforts to respond within two (2) hours to Bank of America's initial request for assistance in correcting or creating a workaround for an Error.
perturbation.changed_text: Supplier shall attempt to respond within a reasonable timeframe to Bank of America's initial request for assistance in addressing an Error.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces multiple ambiguities. Replacing 'two (2) hours' with 'a reasonable timeframe' is vague and subjective. Also, changing 'correcting or creating a workaround' to 'addressing an Error' broadens the scope without clearly defining what constitutes 'addressing'. This introduces uncertainty as to what actions the Supplier is actually obligated to take and by when.
perturbation.law_citation: FFIEC IT Examination Handbook - Outsourcing Technology Services
perturbation.law_explanation: The FFIEC IT Examination Handbook provides guidance for banking regulators to evaluate technology outsourcing. The change weakens the requirement for timely error resolution, creating ambiguity around resolution obligations which could be considered non-compliant under FFIEC guidelines for outsourced technology services.

Search More Search Tools Financial Institution Search CRA Performance Evaluations Search Enforcement Actions Search Corporate Applications Search (CAS) Home News & Events Newsroom OCC Bulletin 2023-17 | June 6, 2023 Third-Party Relationships: Interagency Guidance on Risk Management Share This Page: To Chief Executive Officers of All National Banks, Federal Savings Associations, and Federal Branches and Agencies; Department and Division Heads; All Examining Personnel; and Other Interested Parties Summary The Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC), the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System, and the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (collectively, the agencies) today issued the "Interagency Guidance on Third-Party Relationships: Risk Management." Rescissions This bulletin rescinds 1 OCC Bulletin 2013-29, "Third-Party Relationships: Risk Management Guidance" OCC Bulletin 2020-10, "Third-Party Relationships: Frequently Asked Questions to Supplement OCC Bulletin 2013-29" Note for Community Banks This guidance applies to all banks with third-party relationships. 2 Highlights The final interagency guidance promotes consistency in the agenciesâ supervisory approach to third-party risk management. outlines the third-party risk management life cycle and identifies risk management principles applicable to each stage of the life cycle. clarifies that not all third-party relationships present the same level of risk or criticality to a bankâs operations. describes sound risk management principles to consider when developing and implementing third-party risk management practices, commensurate with the bankâs risk profile and complexity as well as the criticality of the activity supported by the third party. Further Information Please contact Tamara Culler, Director for Governance and Operational Risk Policy, Operational Risk Policy Division, at (202) 649-6550. Grovetta N. Gardineer Senior Deputy Comptroller for Bank Supervision Policy Related Link " Interagency Guidance on Third-Party Relationships: Risk Management " 1 OCC Bulletin 2002-16, "Bank Use of Foreign-Based Third-Party Service Providers: Risk Management Guidance," and other OCC issuances and publications that discuss the use of third parties are not being rescinded by this bulletin and instead supplement the final interagency guidance. 2 "Banks" refers collectively to national banks, federal savings associations, covered savings associations, and federal branches and agencies of foreign banking organizations. Topic(s): Bank Information Technology (BIT) Bank-Fintech Arrangements Supervision of Third Party Technology Service Providers - BIT Third Party Risk Management
perturbation.justification: The change from 'two (2) hours' to 'a reasonable timeframe' and from 'correcting or creating a workaround' to 'addressing an Error' introduces unacceptable ambiguity regarding the time frame and scope of the Supplier's obligation. The OCC bulletin emphasizes the importance of managing third-party risk, which implicitly includes timely and effective error resolution. By weakening these requirements, the changed text contradicts the spirit of risk management outlined in the OCC guidance, potentially creating a situation where timely resolution cannot be assured. The original text had clear guidelines, while the changed text does not provide that assurance, going against the need to properly manage third-party relationships

perturbation.original_text: The diagrams shall show the detail of the system architecture including, without limitation, the logical topology of routers, switches, internet firewalls, management or monitoring firewalls, servers (web, application and database), intrusion detection systems, network and platform redundancy. The diagrams shall include all hosting environments including those provided by Supplier's Subcontractors.
perturbation.changed_text: The diagrams should generally depict the system architecture, including the logical arrangement of network components, but specific details regarding subcontractor hosting environments may be provided upon request.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall show the detail' to 'should generally depict' weakens the mandate for comprehensive diagrams. The phrase 'logical arrangement of network components' is less specific than listing routers, switches, etc. Adding 'but specific details regarding subcontractor hosting environments may be provided upon request' makes providing subcontractor information optional rather than mandatory. This reduces the level of required transparency.
perturbation.law_explanation: OCC Bulletin 2013-29 emphasizes the need for banks to manage risks associated with third-party relationships, including subcontractors. By making the provision of details regarding subcontractor hosting environments optional, the change reduces the bank's ability to properly assess and manage the risks associated with those subcontractors, potentially violating OCC guidelines.

perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the requirement for detailed diagrams of the system architecture, including subcontractor hosting environments, potentially hindering the bank's ability to manage third-party risks. The original text mandates detailed diagrams, while the changed text makes providing details about subcontractor hosting environments optional. The OCC Bulletin 2013-29 (rescinded by OCC Bulletin 2023-17 but still relevant as historical context for the law explanation) emphasizes managing risks associated with third-party relationships. By making subcontractor details optional, the revised text weakens the ability to assess and manage those risks, creating a contradiction. The scraped snippet mentions 'Interagency Guidance on Third-Party Relationships: Risk Management,' which promotes a consistent approach to third-party risk management. The modified contract language undermines this guidance by reducing required transparency regarding subcontractors.
